coredata.dc:description: Achieving rapid hemostasis and highly effective antibacterial holds significant importance in the early-stage treatment of wounds. In this study, a hybrid aerogel patch comprising carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified 2-dimensional (2D) porphyrinic metal–organic framework (MOF) nanosheets was designed by incorporating gelatin methacrylate (GelMA) and polyacrylamide (PAM) based matrix. On one hand, CQDs were stably doped onto the surface of the 2D MOF nanosheets, thereby enhancing the photodynamic activity through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. After the preparation of hybrid aerogel patch, the patch loaded with CQDs-doped 2D MOF exhibited excellent photodynamic bactericidal activity against Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (>99.99 %) and Gram-negative Escherichia coli (>99.99 %). On the other hand, the hybrid patch with highly porous and absorbent structure can rapidly absorb blood to aggregate clotting components and form a hydration barrier covering the wound to enhance hemostasis. Besides, the hemolysis and cytotoxicity assays demonstrated a good biocompatibility of this designed patch. In summary, this 2D MOF-loaded aerogel patch holds a potential to achieve rapid hemostasis and effective anti-infection in the early-stage treatment of traumatic wounds.

head.ce:title: A fast hemostatic and enhanced photodynamic 2-dimensional metal-organic framework loaded aerogel patch for wound management

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory of Eco-textiles, Ministry of Education, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Achieving rapid hemostasis and highly effective antibacterial holds significant importance in the early-stage treatment of wounds. In this study, a hybrid aerogel patch comprising carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified 2-dimensional (2D) porphyrinic metal–organic framework (MOF) nanosheets was designed by incorporating gelatin methacrylate (GelMA) and polyacrylamide (PAM) based matrix. On one hand, CQDs were stably doped onto the surface of the 2D MOF nanosheets, thereby enhancing the photodynamic activity through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. After the preparation of hybrid aerogel patch, the patch loaded with CQDs-doped 2D MOF exhibited excellent photodynamic bactericidal activity against Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (>99.99 %) and Gram-negative Escherichia coli (>99.99 %). On the other hand, the hybrid patch with highly porous and absorbent structure can rapidly absorb blood to aggregate clotting components and form a hydration barrier covering the wound to enhance hemostasis. Besides, the hemolysis and cytotoxicity assays demonstrated a good biocompatibility of this designed patch. In summary, this 2D MOF-loaded aerogel patch holds a potential to achieve rapid hemostasis and effective anti-infection in the early-stage treatment of traumatic wounds.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Every year, millions of people suffer from intentional or unintentional injuries because of the war, traffic accident, surgery, or abrasions, which can lead to excessive bleeding, infection, chronic wounds, and even death in severe cases [1,2]. The healing process of a traumatic wound begins with vasoconstriction of blood vessels and initiation of coagulation cascade, which helps to form a thrombus and stop bleeding at the wound site (hemostatic step). Subsequently, the process follows a series of immune responses to detect and eliminate these identified foreign invaders or damaged cells (inflammatory step). Finally, the removal of adverse factors at the wound site facilitates the granulation and dermal remodeling phases, leading to the wound contraction and new tissues formation in the next few days (granulation and dermal remodeling steps) [3–6]. In this case, achieving hemostasis and anti-inflammation are absolutely vital for the ultimate recovery of the wound [7,8].

In response to the hemostatic and inflammatory phases, a variety of dressings have been developed for wound management [9,10]. Basically, the major categories of current wound dressings include gauze, film, hydrogel, and sponge [11,12]. Among them, sponges with high porosity are the most conventional form used as wound dressings, as they can not only accelerate local hemostasis by concentrating the blood components and coagulation factors, but also absorb exudate around the wound to keep a better healing environment [13,14]. Gelatin-based sponges manufactured by lyophilization, have been successfully commercialized and remain the most commonly used sponges for wound treatments [15]. However, gelatin-based sponges also present limitations, such as poor mechanical performance, suboptimal durability, or toxic crosslinking processing, making them unsuitable for clinical use [16]. Accordingly, gelatin methacrylate (GelMA), which is modified by grafting methacrylate groups, has been developed to improve the usability of these biomaterials. As a photopolymerized gelatin-based derivative, GelMA contains gelatin as its backbone, which can activate platelets and the expression of coagulation factors in the clotting cascade without triggering immune rejection [17,18]. Furthermore, a number of cell-responsive peptide structures in GelMA molecular provide many appropriate cell adhesion sites for cell growth and proliferation [19,20]. Although these characteristics all show the great potential of GelMA in wound hemostasis and healing phases, for the fabrication of an ideal wound dressing, antimicrobial properties should also be considered.

In order to acquire the desirable antimicrobial function, a series of new biocidal mechanisms have been proposed in the past decades, such as photodynamic, photothermal, and ultrasound-driven inactivation [21,22]. In particular, photodynamic therapy (PDT) with nonspecific and highly efficient properties has been widely applied in antitumor and antibacterial applications [23,24]. Mechanistically, PDT relies on the photocatalytic reactions to generate the reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can destroy the membrane structure of microorganisms, while avoiding the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria [25]. In recent years, CuTCPP nanosheets, as a 2-dimensional (2D) porphyrinic metal–organic framework (MOF), was developed as an ideal PDT candidate for the treatment of various clinical diseases. For instance, researchers have been exploring the use of CuTCPP nanosheets for cancer therapy, bacteria-infected wound healing, and periodontitis management, because of its wide applicability and excellent biocompatibility [26–28]. Besides, this nanosheet-structured MOF with a relatively large surface area possesses advantages such as abundant active sites, excellent light-harvesting networks, and rapid energy transfer [28]. Nevertheless, the low efficiency of ROS generation also limits the application of CuTCPP nanosheets. Fortunately, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) may provide a feasible strategy to enhance the photodynamic activity of 2D CuTCPP nanosheets. Specifically, the FRET process relies on the principle that energy from an excited donor is non-radiatively transferred to a nearby acceptor (distance < 10 nm), causing an increase in the excited-state energy of the acceptor [29]. According to previous studies, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted considerable attention in biomedical applications due to their fluorescence quantum yields, abundant functional groups on their surface, and good biocompatibility [30]. Therefore, we proposed that CQDs with specific photoluminescent characteristic could be used as the excited donors and anchored on the CuTCPP surface, to enable the occurrence of FRET process.

To the best of our knowledge, the majority of MOF-based aerogels have been primarily focused on applications such as energy storage, catalysis, sensing, and adsorption, with limited reports on their application in wound management [31–33]. In this work, CQDs were selected as the energy donor and doped on CuTCPP nanosheets surface by solvothermal method to achieve photodynamic enhancement through FRET process. The CQDs-doped CuTCPP (CuTCPP@CQDs) nanosheets were systematically analyzed by morphological observations and structural characterizations. In addition, the photophysical and photodynamic characterizations were conducted to confirm the feasibility of this FRET strategy. Following that, the synthesized CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets were dispersed in a GelMA and polyacrylamide (PAM) based matrix, followed by UV cross-linking and freeze-drying processes to obtain the 2D MOF-loaded aerogel patches. The obtained patches can rapidly uptake the blood from bleeding wounds to form a flexible barrier in situ, which not only can aggregate coagulation components but also activate the platelets to stimulate clot formation. Besides, the antibacterial efficiency of these 2D MOF-loaded patches against Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus and Gram-negative Escherichia coli was evaluated under a simulated solar irradiation (Xenon lamp, λ > 420 nm). The rabbit red blood cells and rat fibroblast cells were also utilized to confirm the hemocompatibility and cytocompatibility. All of these results demonstrated the efficient photodynamic bactericidal activity, rapid hemostasis ability, and good biocompatibility of the fabricated 2D MOF-loaded patches, which make them a promising assisting medical dressing for early wound management.

Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (99 %), 4,4′,4′′,4′′′-(Porphine-5,10,15,20-tetrayl)tetrakis(benzoic acid) (TCPP, 97 %), trifluoroacetic acid, and 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China. Poly(ethylene glycol) diacrylate (PEGDA, Mn = 750) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidone (TEMPONE) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China. N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol, glutaraldehyde (50 %), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Mw = 40,000), citric acid, 1,2-ethylenediamine, PAM (Mw = 3,000,000), and other chemicals were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China. GelMA (DS = 70 %) was synthesized following a previous report [34]. The fresh rabbit blood containing anticoagulant was purchased from Qingdao Antiserum Biotechnology Co., Ltd, China.

CQDs were synthesized by a microwave method. Typically, 0.5 g citric acid was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water, and 2 mL 1,2-ethylenediamine was slowly dropped in this solution with stirring. Then, the mixture was transferred into a 700 W microwave oven and heated for 2 min. After that, the reacted mixture was naturally cooled down to room temperature and diluted in 30 mL deionized water. Subsequently, the dilution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 30 min to remove insoluble impurities and obtain the supernatant CQDs suspension. The CQDs suspension was diluted 5 times and dialyzed in deionized water using a dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 Da) for 24 h. At last, the dialyzed CQDs suspension was frozen and dried by a freezing dryer to collect the solid CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CuTCPP and CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: CuTCPP nanosheets were synthesized according to a previous method with slight modification [28]. In detail, 14.4 mg Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, 12 μL trifluoroacetic acid, 10 mg PVP, and 16 mg TCPP were successively added in a flask containing 64 mL DMF/ethanol (v/v, 3:1) solvent, and sonicated to completely dissolve. The flask was heated at 80 °C with constant stirring (500 rpm) for 4 h. After the reaction, the flask with obtained mixture was naturally cooled down to room temperature, then, the mixture was centrifuged at 8,000 rpm and washed with ethanol for 3 times. Finally, the collected CuTCPP nanosheets were dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 24 h and kept in the dark. Similarly, before the solvothermal reaction, 1 mL of CQDs/DMF solution (1 mg/mL) was dropped into the flask containing these reactants to prepare CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets.

GelMA solution (10 wt%) and PAM solution (10 wt%) were prepared separately in deionized water. The GelMA solution and the PAM solution were mixed at a ratio of 7:3 v/v under stirring, then PEGDA (2 % v/v) and photoinitiator (Irgacure 2959, 0.3 % w/v) was added to the GelMA/PAM mixture with sonication and constant stirring to prepare precursor solution. Meanwhile, the synthesized CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets were sonicated in deionized water for 30 min to obtain fragmented CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets suspension (1 mg/mL). After that, an equal volume of the fragmented CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets suspension was mixed with above precursor solution to obtain the GelMA/PEGDA/PAM/CuTCPP@CQDs (GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs) formulation. Next, 0.5 mL of the formulation was added to a 24-well plate and irradiated under UV light (10 W, 365 nm). After 15 min of photopolymerization, the cured product was washed in deionized water and subsequently freeze-dried to prepare GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patch. The GPP/CuTCPP patch was prepared by the same method. Additionally, in preliminary experiments, GelMA/PEGDA, GelMA/PAM, and GelMA/PEGDA/PAM (GPP) patches were prepared by the similar method. In detail, the GelMA solution (10 wt%) containing PEGDA (2 % v/v) and photoinitiator (0.3 % w/v) was mixed with an equal volume of deionized water to prepare GelMA/PEGDA, the GelMA and PAM solution (mixed at a ratio of 7:3 v/v) containing photoinitiator (0.3 % w/v) was mixed with an equal volume of deionized water to prepare GelMA/PAM, the GelMA and PAM solution (mixed at a ratio of 7:3 v/v) containing PEGDA (2 % v/v) and photoinitiator (0.3 % w/v) was mixed with an equal volume of deionized water to prepare GPP.

The scanning electron microscope (SEM, SU1510, HITACHI, Japan), scanning electron microscope (SEM, SU1510, HITACHI, Japan), and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, HITACHI, Japan) were conducted for morphological characterization of the samples. Nano Measurer software (Fudan University, China) was utilized to measure the average diameters and size distributions. The structural information of the samples was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, NEXUS 470, Nicolet, USA) and X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D2 PHASER, BRUKER, Germany). The zeta potential values were recorded by a zeta potential analyzer (Nano ZS90, Malvern, UK). The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was characterized using an Octane Elect EDS System (EDS-30, AMETEK, USA). The UV–visible absorbance and photoluminescence spectra were recorded by UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, HITACHI, Japan), respectively. Tensile tester (INSTRON 1185, Instron, USA) was employed to evaluated the mechanical property in a 40 % constant humidity.

Electron spin resonance spectrometry (ESR, Bruker EMXplus-10/12, Germany) and DPBF method were used to measure the 1O2 generation rate and evaluate the photodynamic activity of the samples. For ESR, the CuTCPP or CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets were sonically suspended in deionized water at a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL, then 0.5 mL of the suspension was uniformly mixed with 0.5 mL of TEMPONE/H2O solution (100 mmol/L). After that, the mixture was illuminated under a Xenon Lamp (500 W, λ ≥ 420 nm, 15 cm) for 30 min and subsequently detected by ESR to record the 1O2 signal. Regarding the DPBF detection, 0.5 mL of CuTCPP or CuTCPP@CQDs aqueous suspension (0.2 mg/mL) was mixed with 3 mL of DPBF/DMF solution (40 mg/L) in a quartz cuvette, then the mixture was exposed to an illumination (a 520 nm laser) for 5 min and recorded by a UV–visible spectrophotometer at 1 min intervals from 350 to 500 nm wavelength.

To investigate the 1O2 production of fabricated patches, the cylindrical sample (Ф = 14 mm, H = 3 mm) was uniformly cut into four equal parts and one of them was added to a quartz cuvette. Then 3.5 mL of 50 % ethanol containing DPBF (40 mg/L) was added to the quartz cuvette. After 4 h immersing in the dark, the cuvette was exposed to a laser (520 nm) and the UV–vis spectrum (350–500 nm) was recorded every 2 min.

Before the test, the fabricated patches were weighed and recorded as M0 . Then the patches were immersed in 37 °C anticoagulated blood for 10 s, and carefully wiped with filter paper to remove the excess blood. The patches after blood absorption ware weight and recorded as M1 . The blood uptake ratio was calculated according to Eq. (1):

To further observe the blood uptake rate of the patches, 5 μL of anticoagulated blood was gently dropped onto the surface of the patches, and the absorption process was recorded with a video camera.

The mechanical performances of the fabricated patches were measured using an INSTRON 1185 tensile tester (Instron, USA) with a 5 mm gauge length, at a constant speed of 20 mm/min and at room temperature. To evaluate the mechanical performances of the hydrated patches, these fabricated patches were immersed in PBS solution at 37 °C for 3 h to form stable hydrogels, which were then tested using the tensile tester.

The porosity of the fabricated patches was measured using a liquid displacement method [35]. Briefly, the as-prepared samples were immersed in ethanol until it reached saturation, and the weights of the samples before and after immersion were recorded as W0 and W1 , respectively. The porosity ratio was calculated by using Eq. (2):

Where ρ and V represent the density of ethanol and the volume of the as-prepared patches, respectively.

For the swelling test, the samples were freeze-dried to obtain a dry weight (W0 ), then these lyophilized samples were transferred to PBS solution and incubated in 37 °C water for 48 h. Every interval (5 min, 10 min, 1 h, 2 h, 4 h, 8 h, 24 h, 48 h), each sample was carefully wiped with filter paper and weighted as Wt . The swelling ratio was calculated according to Eq. (3):

Regarding the degradation test, the lyophilized samples were weighted (W0 ) and fed into plastic tubes, then 4 mL of PBS was added into each tube and placed in an incubator at 37 °C. At set intervals, the liquid in the corresponding tube was removed, and the tube was dried using a freeze-dryer. The dried samples were taken out and weighed as Wt , and the degradation ratio was calculated using the Eq. (4):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial efficiency and SEM observation of bacterial morphology
ce:section.ce:para: The bactericidal performance of GPP, GPP/CuTCPP, GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches against Gram-positive S. aureus (ATCC 6538) and Gram-negative E. coli (ATCC 43895) were investigated according to AATCC 100–2004 Test Method with slight modification [36,37]. Briefly, a quarter of the prepared patch was placed into a sterile 24-well plate, then 50 μL of bacterial suspension was dropped on the patch for 1 h incubation under illumination (Xenon lamp, 500 W, λ ≥ 420 nm, 15 cm) or in dark. After that, 2 mL of PBS solution was added to the well to resuspend the bacteria from the patch, and subsequently the bacterial resuspension solution was serially diluted for 3 times (10, 100, 1000-fold). Finally, 50 μL of each bacterial resuspension at different concentrations was evenly dropped on agar plates, and incubated for 16–24 h at 37 °C for counting. All the test groups were performed in triplicate.

In addition, the bacteria were inoculated on the patches and illuminated for 30 min to investigate the biocidal mechanism. In brief, the illuminated patches were immersed in glutaraldehyde solution (2.5 %) overnight at 4 °C for bacterial fixation. Then, the patches were dehydrated sequentially in a series of ethanol solutions (50 %, 60 %, 70 %, 80 %, 90 %, and 100 %, v/v) and dried naturally. The dried samples were sputter-coated with gold before SEM observation.

A series of in vitro hemostasis experiments were performed to evaluate the hemostatic performance of GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs sample, containing blood clotting index (BCI), in vitro clotting time, and SEM observation of blood cell adhesion. Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) was collected from yellowish middle layer of the anticoagulant whole blood after centrifugation (1700 rpm, 10 min).

For BCI test, a cylindrical sample (Ф = 14 mm, H = 3 mm) was placed in a Petri dish and preheated in a 37 °C air oscillator for 5 min. Meanwhile, 0.2 mL of CaCl2 solution (0.1 mol/L) was mixed with 1.8 mL of preheated anticoagulant whole blood for 10 s vortex. Then, 0.1 mL of above blood was dropped onto the sample and incubated in the air oscillator for 5 min. Subsequently, 20 mL of deionized water was slowly added in the Petri dish with slightly shaking (30 rpm). After 10 min, the solution in Petri dish was measured at 540 nm by UV–vis spectrophotometer and recorded as Abssample . As a blank control, the solution in Petri dish without sample was recorded as Absbalnk . The BCI value was calculated by using Eq. (5):
(5)

In vitro blood clotting time test was also performed to evaluate the hemostatic performance. In brief, adding 0.2 mL CaCl2 solution (0.1 mol/L) into preheated anticoagulant whole blood and vortexing for 10 s, meanwhile, the samples (10 mg) were placed into 24-well plate and preheated at 37 °C. Then, 50 μL of above blood was dropped onto each sample for contact. Every 30 s, PBS solution was gently added to each well and pipetted to remove the unclotted blood components. The time at which a uniform and stable blood clot formed in the wells after adding the PBS solution was defined as the blood clotting time.

To further investigate the formation of blood clot, 0.1 mL of anticoagulant whole blood or PRP was dropped onto GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs sample and incubated at 37 °C. After 5 min, the sample was slight washed with PBS solution for 3 times, then soaked in 2.5 wt% glutaraldehyde solution at 4 °C overnight, and gradually dehydrated in 50 %, 60 %, 70 %, 80 %, 90 %, and 100 % ethanol solutions. After being naturally dried, the sample was characterized using SEM.

The hemocompatibility was assessed by hemolysis assay [38]. Before this assay, the anticoagulant whole blood (5 mL) was mixed with PBS solution (10 mL) and centrifuged (1500 rpm, 10 min) to collect the red blood cells (RBCs), then the RBCs were diluted with PBS solution at ratio of 1:19 (v/v). After that, 1 mL of the diluted RBCs solution was added in 2 mL PBS solution containing 10 mg samples and co-incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. Meanwhile, the diluted RBCs solution was added in deionized water and PBS solution for 12 h incubation, which were used as positive and negative groups, respectively. Afterwards, the incubated mixtures were centrifuged (2000 rpm, 10 min) and the supernatants were recorded at 540 nm by a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The hemolysis ratio was calculated by using Eq. (6):
(6)

Where Asample , Anegative , and Apositive represent the absorbance values of the supernatant collected from samples, PBS, and deionized water, respectively.

The biocompatibility was evaluated using cytotoxicity assay (CCK-8 method) [39]. In detail, L929 cells were added to a 96-well plate containing growth medium and incubated in 5 % CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C, meanwhile, the sterile samples (10 mg) were extracted in another 96-well plate containing growth medium at 37 °C. After 24 h, the growth medium of L929 cells was slowly removed and replaced with sample extraction medium, and then incubated for 24 h sequentially. The CCK-8 reagent was added to each well and the absorbance was recorded at 540 nm using a microplate reader. L929 cells incubated in growth medium without sample extraction was used as a positive control. In addition, the live and dead L929 cells were further observed by using fluorescent staining method. Before adding the CCK-8 reagent, the L929 cells in each well were washed with PBS solution, then stained with diluted Calcein-AM and incubated in dark for 15 min. After that, these cells were washed with PBS solution 3 times and stained with diluted propidium iodide for 5 min incubation in dark. Finally, the live or dead cells can be observed by an inverted fluorescence microscope.

The statistical data were analyzed by using ANOVA and Student’s t-test, and the differences can be considered statistically significant when P < 0.05. Data were shown as the mean ± standard deviation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis diagram of CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets is illustrated in Fig. 1 a. To reveal the successful preparation of CuTCPP@CQDs and the feasibility of photodynamic enhancement, the obtained products were systematically characterized.

As shown in Fig. S1, the chemical structures of synthesized CQDs, CuTCPP, and CuTCPP@CQDs were characterized by FTIR spectra. Specifically, in CQDs spectrum, a broad band from 3600 cm−1 to 3000 cm−1 was observed, which was caused by OH and NH stretching vibration [40]. The peaks around 2900 cm−1 were ascribed to CH stretching vibration. The stretching vibration of CO could be observed at 1700 cm−1. A peak appeared at 1650 cm−1, corresponding to the skeletal vibrations of CC. The bending vibration of NH could be observed around 1560 cm−1 [41]. Besides, the peak around 1290 cm−1 was ascribed to CN stretching vibration. Thus, it could be inferred that abundant carboxyl and amino group presented in CQDs. In CuTCPP spectrum, the two peaks around 2930 cm−1 and 2865 cm−1 were ascribed to stretching vibration of CH bonds in TCPP ligands. The peaks at 1665 cm−1 and 1605 cm−1 corresponded to CO stretching vibration and CC skeletal vibration, respectively. The peaks at 1400 cm−1, 1340 cm−1, and 1000 cm−1 were attributed to CH in-plane bending vibration, CN stretching vibration, and CO stretching vibration, respectively. These peaks are consistent with the previous report, confirming the successful synthesis of CuTCPP. [28]. Meanwhile, the CuTCPP@CQDs spectrum presented same characteristic peaks to CuTCPP spectrum, indicating the CQDs with low loading has no effect to the CuTCPP synthesis. The crystalline structure of these products was also characterized by XRD patterns. As shown in Fig. S2, CuTCPP and CuTCPP@CQDs patterns showed the same typical peaks signed as (110), (200), (210), (320), and (002), which was ascribed to the tetragonal structure of CuTCPP [42]. In CQDs pattern, a broad and weak diffraction peak at 15-30° could be observed, which represented the amorphous graphite structure of the CQDs [43]. Therefore, the FTIR and XRD analyses indicated that the introduction of CQDs has no effect on the synthesis of CuTCPP.

The zeta potential values of CQDs, CuTCPP, and CuTCPP@CQDs dispersed in deionized water were measured. As displayed in Fig. 1b, the prepared CQDs displayed a positive zeta potential (4.2 ± 1.4 mV) in aqueous solution. In addition, the zeta potential of negatively charged CuTCPP nanosheets (-13.7 ± 3.5 mV) increased to −11.3 ± 2.2 mV after loading the positively charged CQDs. Thus, the results confirmed that the positively charged CQDs could be absorbed on negatively charged CuTCPP nanosheets through electrostatic interactions. To further observe the morphology of CQDs, CuTCPP, and CuTCPP@CQDs, the products were characterized by TEM. In Fig. 1c, the image showed that the prepared CQDs were well scattered on the surface with an average size of 2.5 ± 0.5 nm. As displayed in Fig. 1d and e, both CuTCPP and CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets had a relatively large lateral size. However, according to the previous method, the relatively large nanosheets could be separately disassembled into smaller nanosheets by sonication [28]. The CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets sonicated for 30 min had a relatively small lateral size of less than 1 μm (Fig. S3a). Comparing the insets in Fig. 1d and e, a lot of CQDs could be observed on CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets, revealing the successfully and uniformly loading of CQDs on CuTCPP nanosheets. Furthermore, the morphology of the sonicated CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets after immersing in PBS for 20 days was observed using TEM. The image (Fig. S3b) exhibited numerous fragmented products, which could be attributed to the degradation of the nanosheets in PBS [28]. Meanwhile, in the inset, the loaded CQDs could still be observed on the degraded nanosheets, indicating the stable combination of CQDs and CuTCPP.

The fluorescence emission spectrum of CQDs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CuTCPP were further recorded. As shown in Fig. 1f, CQDs displayed a strong emission peak at 450 nm, meanwhile, CuTCPP possessed two characteristic absorption peaks at around 440 nm and 550 nm, respectively. Remarkably, a complete overlap between CQDs emission spectra and CuTCPP absorption spectra can be observed from 400 to 600 nm, providing a theoretical basis for the FRET phenomenon [44]. In addition, the UV–vis absorption spectra of CuTCPP and CuTCPP@CQDs were recorded to compare their ability to absorb light. In Fig. 1g, CuTCPP@CQDs exhibited the same characteristic absorption peaks as CuTCPP around 440 nm and 550 nm, besides, the comparation revealed that the light-harvesting ability of CuTCPP@CQDs was stronger than that of CuTCPP.

The fluorescence spectra, phosphorescence spectra and 1O2 detection of synthesized nanosheets were performed to verify the photophysical and photodynamic enhancements through FRET process. As shown in fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2 a), both CuTCPP and CuTCPP@CQDs displayed red fluorescence signals around 735 nm, while the intensity of CuTCPP@CQDs was higher than that of CuTCPP. The similar phenomenon could also be observed in phosphorescence spectra (Fig. 2b), where CuTCPP@CQDs exhibited stronger luminescence signals in the range from 400 to 700 nm. Thus, the introduction of CQDs enhanced the photoluminescent properties of CuTCPP nanosheets. Furthermore, ESR and DPBF measurements were conducted to detect the 1O2 generation. In ESR spectra (Fig. 2c), CuTCPP@CQDs exhibited stronger 1O2 signals than CuTCPP, illustrating that the 1O2 yield of CuTCPP@CQDs was higher than that of CuTCPP under visible light irradiation. For DPBF measurements, the nanosheets suspensions mixed with DPBF solution under illumination or in dark, were recorded by UV–vis spectrophotometer. In general, DPBF can be used as a specific probe for 1O2 production efficiency, since the probe can be changed to colorless after reacting with 1O2, and cause a decrease in absorbance around 415 nm. As shown in Fig. S4, the absorbance of the mixture containing CuTCPP (a) or CuTCPP@CQDs (b) and DPBF remained unchanged within 5 min, indicating no generation of 1O2 from CuTCPP or CuTCPP@CQDs under dark conditions. However, the absorbance around 415 nm decreased in both Fig. 2d and e, confirming the generation of 1O2 by CuTCPP or CuTCPP@CQDs under light irradiation. Besides, the fitting curves of absorbance changes (415 nm) over time for the four groups were shown in Fig. 2f. The slopes of the fitting curves for the dark groups were close to zero, whereas, the fitting curves of the irradiated groups exhibited slopes of −9.5 × 10−3 and −2.1 × 10−2, respectively. The results demonstrated that CuTCPP@CQDs had a greater ability to generate 1O2 than CuTCPP within 5 min, which was consistent with the ESR result. In summary, the mechanism of photophysical and photodynamic enhancements could be clarified, and the FRET process between CQDs and CuTCPP are schematically illustrated in Fig. 2g. Under illumination, the fluorescence resonance energy produced by the CQDs was non-radiatively transferred to the adjacent CuTCPP nanosheets through dipole–dipole interactions [45]. During this process, the number of electronically excited states of the CuTCPP increased, including excited singlet and excited triplet states. The return of excited singlet states to the ground state was accompanied by fluorescence emission, while the return of excited triplet states to the ground state was accompanied by phosphorescence emission and the conversion of 3O2 to 1O2. Hence, the introduced CQDs can improve the photophysical and photodynamic activities of CuTCPP through the FRET process.

The photocurable formulation was prepared by mixing an aqueous solution of GelMA, non-crosslinked PAM, the cross-linker PEGDA, and photoinitiator. After photopolymerization, the linear polymer (PAM) was entrapped in the cross-linked matrix, forming a semi-interpenetrated network (semi-IPN) system [46]. PAM was selected because of its inherent properties, such as strong water-absorbing capacity, biocompatibility, and the existence of hydroxyl groups capable of forming hydrogen bonds, which make PAM an attractive material for medical applications. In addition, a small amount of the cross-linker PEGDA was added to the precursor formulation to enhance the cross-linking network structure of the semi-IPN system [47]. Thus, in preliminary experiments, the mechanical performances of the fabricated aerogels and rehydrated aerogels were tested to assess the suitability of the formulation system. As shown in Fig. S5, the presence of PEGDA in the patches and rehydrated patches resulted in an improvement of breaking strength, which could be attributed to the increased cross-linking density achieved by the cross-linker [46]. Besides, after adding PAM, the fracture elongation of the patches and rehydrated patches was improved. The presence of non-crosslinked PAM chains in the semi-IPN system reduced intermolecular interactions and increased chain mobility, enabling the polymer chains to adjust their conformation and arrangement to external forces [48]. Furthermore, we also conducted preliminary experiments to determine the effect of PAM component on the blood uptake ability. As exhibited in Fig. S6, the incorporation of PAM in the patches significantly improved the blood absorption ratio. Additionally, the dynamic photography in Fig. S7 confirmed the enhanced blood uptake efficiency of the patches after incorporating PAM. Simultaneously, a slight surface collapse was observed after the GelMA/PAM patch absorbed the blood, demonstrating that the blending of PEGDA can improve the structural stability of the patches. Rapid absorption of blood is beneficial for hemostatic materials to concentrate the clotting factors, achieving the goal of fast hemostasis [49]. Based on the preliminary experiments, PAM and PEGDA were introduced into the GelMA-based formulation system to further fabricate GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches.

In the preceding section, CuTCPP nanosheets were successfully modified with CQDs, achieving photodynamic enhancement through FRET. Subsequently, the modified nanosheets were incorporated into above-designed photocurable formulation to fabricate photodynamic antimicrobial patches. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, after exposure to UV light, the liquid blended formulation cured, forming the semi-IPN hydrogel and simultaneously immobilizing the nanosheets in situ. Through a freeze-drying process, the hydrogel transformed into a porous aerogel patch, and the average pore diameter of the prepared GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patch was 100–200 μm. Comparing the surface morphology of GPP patch to that of GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patch, it was observed that the latter exhibits a rougher texture. This increased roughness could be attributed to the incorporation of insoluble CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets. EDS analysis also confirmed the porous structure of GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patch (Fig. 3e). Meanwhile, the presence of four elements (C, N, O, Cu) on its surface was detected and displayed in their respective mapping images. Fig. 3f shown the signal peaks of C, N, O, and Cu at 0.25 keV, 0.52 keV, 2.38 keV, and 1.02 keV, respectively. And the corresponding contents were measured as 45 %, 14 %, 35 %, and 6 % for C, N, O, and Cu, respectively.

The mechanical properties of the GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patch and the hydrated GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patch are shown in Fig. 3g and h. The results indicated that the incorporation of CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets led to a decrease in both the fracture strength and fracture elongation of the patch or hydrated patch. This phenomenon could be attributed to the influence of the dispersed phase (nanosheets) on the mechanical performance of the continuous phase (patch matrixes) [33]. Fig. 3i exhibited that the porosity of GPP and GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches was measured to be 78.3 ± 1.9 % and 76.2 ± 4.8 %, respectively. The result suggested that the introduction of CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets had a negligible impact on the porosity of the patches. The blood uptake ratio test revealed that the designed patches, after the incorporation of CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets, exhibited a slight decrease in blood absorption efficiency, while still maintaining a remarkable level of 522.8 ± 6.6 % (Fig. 3j). Similarly, the GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patch displayed slightly lower swelling ratios than the GPP patch, but still possessed strong water-absorption capacity (Fig. 3k). In addition, the degradation performances of GPP and GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S8, both the GPP and GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches exhibited good degradation ability and were degraded over 70 % after 30 days. Interestingly, a slightly delayed degradation rate of GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs than GPP were observed, which could be attributed to the inhibitory effect of copper ions on microbial activity [50]. This inhibition caused by copper ions would be further demonstrated in antibacterial analysis.

The 1O2 yield of the fabricated GPP, GPP/CuTCPP, and GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches were detected by using DPBF probe. As shown in Fig. S9, the patches containing MOF nanosheets exhibited significant photodynamic activity. Specifically, GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs had a greater ability of 1O2 generation than GPP/CuTCPP, which was consistent with the results obtained from the MOF nanosheets photodynamic testing. Based on the results of photodynamic activity, the antibacterial performance of the patches against S. aureus and E. coli was evaluated under visible light for 1 h illumination. The bacterial suspensions without contact with the patches were used as the blank groups. As exhibited in Fig. 4 a and b, the antibacterial efficiency of the blank groups indicated that the bactericidal effect of 1 h exposure to visible light could be considered negligible. The GPP patches displayed the reduction of 10.42 % (Dark) and 29.69 % (Light) against S. aureus and 10.48 % (Dark) and 18.10 % (Light) against E. coli, respectively. This insignificant decrease could be ascribed to the bacterial adhesion between bacteria and the patches, rather than the photodynamic inactivation [21]. Compared with the blank groups and GPP patches, GPP/CuTCPP and GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs exhibited effective photodynamic antibacterial activities under visible light illumination for 1 h. To be specific, the GPP/CuTCPP patches displayed moderate antibacterial efficiency of 97.39 % against S. aureus and 97.33 % against E. coli, respectively. Comparatively, the GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches showed higher photodynamic antibacterial efficiency against S. aureus (>99.99 %) and E. coli (>99.99 %). Interestingly, under dark condition, both GPP/CuTCPP (S. aureus: 50.52 % reduction, E. coli: 48.09 %) and GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs (S. aureus: 56.77 % reduction, E. coli: 40.01 %) patches seemed to exhibit slight antibacterial activities against the bacteria, which could be attributed to the release of copper ions from the blended nanosheets. [51] In addition, the survival colonies of S. aureus and E. coli on agar plates (Fig. 4c and d) after the antibacterial testing also displayed the corresponding results.

To explore the photodynamic inactivation mechanism, the two kinds of bacteria (S. aureus and E. coli) were inoculated on these patches (GPP, GPP/CuTCPP, and GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs) and treated under visible light for 30 min, followed by morphological observations using SEM. As shown in Fig. 4e, S. aureus on the GPP patch showed an intact spherical shape, whereas on the GPP/CuTCPP and GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches, noticeable shrinkage and deformation of S. aureus membranes could be observed. Notably, the GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patch induced more pronounced shrinkage and deformation in S. aureus compared to the GPP/CuTCPP patch. Similarly, the SEM images of E. coli (Fig. 4f) exhibited consistent morphological changes on these patches. On the GPP patch, E. coli presented a rod-shaped morphology with intact bacterial membranes. In contrast, the membranes of E. coli were moderately damaged and severely damaged on the GPP/CuTCPP and GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches, respectively.

To sum up, compared to the GPP/CuTCPP patch, the GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patch has higher efficacy in generating 1O2. This heightened 1O2 generation confers a remarkable enhancement in the photodynamic antibacterial efficiency of the GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patch. Moreover, due to the enhanced photodynamic activity, the GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patch could efficiently generate more 1O2 to destroy the bacterial membrane and ultimately lead to bacterial apoptosis under visible light irradiation.

As previously mentioned, the fabricated GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches demonstrated a remarkable capacity to rapidly absorb a substantial volume of blood. Such characteristic is highly advantageous for hemostatic materials, as it facilitates the concentration of coagulation components in the blood, thereby achieving effective hemostasis. Based on this, we evaluated the in vitro hemostatic performance of GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches, while GPP patches and MGS were used for comparison.

To investigate the dynamic blood absorption behavior of these patches, blood droplets were placed on the patches surface and recorded using a high-definition camera, with a commercial gelatin sponge (MGS) serving as the control (Fig. S10). The results revealed that the designed patches exhibited superior blood absorption efficiency compared to the MGS. Fig. 5 a exhibits the BCI values of different samples at 1 min, 3 min, and 5 min after contacting with blood. In general, a lower BCI value represents a higher degree of blood coagulation, whereas a higher BCI value represents a lower degree of blood coagulation. Therefore, it could be inferred that varying degrees of coagulation reactions occurred after the blood contacting with MGS, GPP, and GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs. Due to the higher blood absorption efficiency for concentrating clotting components, GPP and GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches exhibited better coagulation performance than MGS. It was worth noting that the BCI values further decreased after introducing CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets into the GPP-based patches. The right insets in Fig. 5a displayed the photographs of blood/deionized water solutions corresponding to the BCI values, where a clearer solution indicated more efficient coagulation of the sample. To investigate the mechanism of clot formation, whole blood, RBCs, and platelets were individually exposed to GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches, and the interactions were observed using SEM. Fig. 5b illustrated the effective aggregation of blood components within the pores of the GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patch. Additionally, the surface of the patch exhibited a strong adhesion to both RBCs (Fig. 5c) and platelets (Fig. 5d). The magnified image of platelet suggested it could be activated on the patch surface, as evidenced by the extension of pseudopodia. These extended pseudopodia play a vital role in forming a stable blood clot through aggregating the blood components [52].

The in vitro hemostatic abilities of MGS, GPP, and GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches were evaluated by recording their blood clotting time in 24-well plates. As shown in Fig. 5e, the blank group revealed that the clotting time of whole blood without contact with hemostatic materials was approximately 7 min, which was consistent with previous reports. [49,53] After employing MGS, the clotting time was reduced to around 6 min. Notably, after employing GPP and GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches, the clotting time was dramatically reduced to 2.0 ± 0.4 min and 1.2 ± 0.2 min, respectively. As expected, the hemostatic ability of GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patch was better than that of GPP patch, which was consistent with the results of BCI testing.

Thus, GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches exhibited outstanding clotting performance due to the rapid water/blood absorption capacity and platelet activation. Moreover, in comparation between GPP and GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches, the latter showed higher clotting efficiency, potentially related to an accelerated coagulation cascade reaction facilitated by copper ions. [54] Overall, the hemostatic mechanism of GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches can be clarified (Fig. 5f). Upon contact with a bleeding wound, this patch could rapidly absorb the blood to concentrate coagulation factors and accelerate the coagulation cascade. Simultaneously, a small amount of released copper ions from the hybrid patch could further accelerate the clot formation. Eventually, a flexible physical hemostatic barrier would form in situ at the wound site, which could adhere to the traumatic tissue and effectively stop bleeding.

Medical materials used for wound tissue should be biocompatible [55,56]. Therefore, the hemocompatibility and cytocompatibility of GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches were evaluated by using in vitro hemolysis and cytotoxicity assay. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the hemolysis ratios of GPP and GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches were below the 5 % dash line, which is considered an accepted safety standards for blood compatible materials. [53] The photographs on the right displayed the supernatants corresponding to the hemolysis ratios of each group. Clearly, supernatants obtained from GPP and GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs groups exhibited the comparable level of transparency to that of the negative (PBS), indicating their favorable hemocompatibility. However, rupture and hemolysis of RBCs were observed after co-incubation with deionized water. For cytotoxicity assay, the GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches also showed good cytocompatibility. In Fig. 6b, the cell viability of GPP and GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs were determined to be 103.3 ± 1.5 % and 92.2 ± 4.8 %, respectively, both exceeding the 80 % value which is widely recognized as an indicator of biocompatible materials. [57] Moreover, live and dead L929 cells after co-incubation with the specimens were observed by fluorescent staining. In Fig. 6c, majority of the live cells in each group were stained green, while few dead cells were stained red. Compared with blank and GPP group, more dead cells were observed in GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs group, suggesting that the reduced compatibility might be due to the presence of the CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets. Nevertheless, based on these results, the GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patches were demonstrated to be biocompatible and could be used for wound tissue managements.

In conclusion, our study demonstrated that CQDs doped into CuTCPP nanosheets can successfully induce the FRET process and achieve photodynamic enhancement. And, the CuTCPP@CQDs nanosheets were integrated into a semi-IPN system to successfully fabricate a porous 2D MOF-loaded aerogel patch (GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs). Upon contacting with the bleeding wound, this patch can rapidly absorb the blood and form an adhesive physical hydrogel barrier, effectively covering the wound tissue and preventing further bleeding. Meanwhile, the hydrogel barrier formed in situ can activate platelets and facilitate coagulation cascade, further enhancing hemostasis. Furthermore, this GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patch exhibited enhanced photodynamic antibacterial activity against S. aureus (>99.99 %) and E. coli (>99.99 %) within 1 h under light illumination. The in vitro hemolysis and cytotoxicity assays also confirmed the good biocompatibility of the GPP/CuTCPP@CQDs patch. Taken together, the designed 2D MOF-loaded aerogel patch exhibits remarkable hemostatic ability and photodynamic antibacterial activity, making it highly promising for wound management applications. In future work, we’ll continue to develop novel hemostatic and photodynamic antibacterial materials, and further conduct the preclinical investigations to determine their practical application value.
